category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Statistics and Probability/Regression and Correlation

info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Regression and Correlation: simple and multiple linear regression, OLS estimators, ANOVA for regression, R-squared, correlation coefficient, prediction intervals, logistic regression, robust regression.

name.text: STAT23-Q01 OLS Estimators in Simple Linear Regression

questiontext.text: In simple linear regression \(Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \epsilon_i\) with \(\epsilon_i \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(0,\sigma^2)\), the OLS estimators are \(\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{S_{XY}}{S_{XX}}\) and \(\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1\bar{X}\), where \(S_{XY} = \sum(X_i-\bar{X})(Y_i-\bar{Y})\) and \(S_{XX} = \sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2\). These are also the MLEs under normality. The variance of \(\hat{\beta}_1\) is:

feedback.text: \(\hat{\beta}_1 = \sum c_i Y_i\) where \(c_i = (X_i-\bar{X})/S_{XX}\). Since \(\text{Var}(Y_i)=\sigma^2\): \(\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sigma^2\sum c_i^2 = \sigma^2 \cdot S_{XX}/S_{XX}^2 = \sigma^2/S_{XX}\). By Gauss-Markov, this is the BLUE.
This is the variance of \(\bar{Y}\), not the slope estimator.
This is the variance of \(\hat{\beta}_0\) (the intercept), not the slope.
Incorrect; the variance is inversely proportional to the variability in \(X\).

questiontext.text: In simple linear regression, the coefficient of determination is \(R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{Res}}{SS_{Tot}} = \dfrac{SS_{Reg}}{SS_{Tot}}\) where \(SS_{Tot} = \sum(Y_i-\bar{Y})^2\), \(SS_{Res} = \sum(Y_i-\hat{Y}_i)^2\), and \(SS_{Reg} = \sum(\hat{Y}_i-\bar{Y})^2\). In simple linear regression, \(R^2\) equals:

answer.text: \(r^2\) where \(r = S_{XY}/\sqrt{S_{XX}S_{YY}}\) is the sample correlation coefficient

feedback.text: \(SS_{Reg} = \hat{\beta}_1^2 S_{XX} = S_{XY}^2/S_{XX}\) and \(SS_{Tot} = S_{YY}\). So \(R^2 = S_{XY}^2/(S_{XX}S_{YY}) = r^2\). In simple regression, \(R^2\) equals the square of the Pearson correlation coefficient.
\(R^2\) equals \(r^2\), not \(r\). Since \(R^2 \geq 0\) always, it equals \(r^2\), which could be positive even when \(r<0\).
The slope \(\hat{\beta}_1\) has units (units of \(Y\) per unit of \(X\)), while \(R^2\) is dimensionless. They are not equal in general.
This involves the population variance \(\sigma^2\), not the sample quantities. The formula should use \(SS_{Res}\), not \(n\sigma^2\).

questiontext.text: The Gauss-Markov theorem states: in the linear model \(\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}\) with \(E[\boldsymbol{\epsilon}]=\mathbf{0}\) and \(\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\epsilon})=\sigma^2\mathbf{I}\), the OLS estimator \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}\) is BLUE. "BLUE" means:

answer.text: Best Linear Unbiased Estimator — has minimum variance among all linear unbiased estimators

feedback.text: BLUE: (B) Best = minimum variance, (L) Linear = linear function of \(\mathbf{Y}\), (U) Unbiased = \(E[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}]=\boldsymbol{\beta}\). The Gauss-Markov theorem does NOT require normality — only zero mean, constant variance, and uncorrelated errors.

answer.text: Best Linear Unbiased Estimator — has minimum variance among ALL unbiased estimators

feedback.text: BLUE has minimum variance only among LINEAR unbiased estimators. Under normality, OLS is also UMVUE (minimum variance among ALL unbiased estimators), but Gauss-Markov only guarantees the linear class.

answer.text: Best Likelihood Unbiased Estimator — maximizes the likelihood

feedback.text: BLUE stands for "Best Linear Unbiased Estimator," not "Best Likelihood Unbiased Estimator."

answer.text: Best Linear Unbiased Estimator — requires normally distributed errors

feedback.text: The Gauss-Markov theorem does NOT require normality. It only requires uncorrelated errors with constant variance.

questiontext.text: For a bivariate sample \((X_1,Y_1),\ldots,(X_n,Y_n)\), the Pearson correlation coefficient is \(r = \dfrac{S_{XY}}{\sqrt{S_{XX}S_{YY}}}\). Under \(H_0: \rho=0\) (where \(\rho\) is the population correlation), the test statistic \(T = \dfrac{r\sqrt{n-2}}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}\) follows:

feedback.text: Under bivariate normality and \(H_0:\rho=0\), \(T = r\sqrt{n-2}/\sqrt{1-r^2} \sim t_{n-2}\). This follows because \(r\) can be related to the \(t\)-statistic for the slope in regression: \(T = \hat{\beta}_1/SE(\hat{\beta}_1)\).
For testing \(\rho=0\), the \(t_{n-2}\) distribution applies. For testing general \(\rho\neq 0\), Fisher's \(z\)-transformation gives an approximate normal statistic.
The chi-square distribution arises for variance tests and goodness-of-fit, not for the correlation coefficient under \(H_0:\rho=0\).
While \(T^2 \sim F_{1,n-2}\) (which is consistent), the standard form of the test uses \(T \sim t_{n-2}\) for a two-sided test.

questiontext.text: In the multiple regression model \(\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}\) with \(\mathbf{X}\) being \(n\times p\) of rank \(p\) and \(\boldsymbol{\epsilon}\sim N(\mathbf{0},\sigma^2\mathbf{I})\), the OLS estimator \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}\) has the property \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sim\):

feedback.text: \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}\) is a linear transformation of \(\mathbf{Y}\sim N_n(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta},\sigma^2\mathbf{I})\). Hence \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) is multivariate normal with mean \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) and covariance \(\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\).
The covariance matrix is \(\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\), not \(\sigma^2\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}\). Note the inverse.
This is the distribution of \(\mathbf{Y}\), not of \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\). The estimated coefficients are a \(p\)-dimensional vector.
There is no multivariate \(t\)-distribution in this form for OLS estimators. When \(\sigma^2\) is estimated, marginal components of \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) follow \(t_{n-p}\) distributions.

questiontext.text: In multiple regression with \(n=30\) observations and \(p=4\) predictors (including intercept, so \(k=3\) regressors), the regression sum of squares is \(SS_R = 450\) and residual sum of squares is \(SS_e = 150\). The \(F\)-statistic for testing \(H_0:\beta_1=\beta_2=\beta_3=0\) is:

feedback.text: Regression df is the number of regressors (excluding intercept) = 3, not 4 (total parameters).
Residual df = \(n - p = 30 - 4 = 26\), where \(p=4\) includes the intercept.
The \(F\)-statistic uses mean squares (SS divided by df), not the raw sums of squares.

questiontext.text: In simple linear regression with \(n\) observations, at a new point \(x_0\), the 95% confidence interval for the mean response \(E[Y|X=x_0]\) is \(\hat{y}_0 \pm t_{n-2,0.025} \cdot s\sqrt{h_{00}}\) where \(h_{00} = \dfrac{1}{n}+\dfrac{(x_0-\bar{x})^2}{S_{XX}}\). The 95% prediction interval for a new observation \(Y_0\) at \(x_0\) differs because:

answer.text: It adds \(1\) to the standard error factor: \(\hat{y}_0 \pm t_{n-2,0.025} \cdot s\sqrt{1+h_{00}}\)

feedback.text: The prediction error \(Y_0 - \hat{Y}_0\) has variance \(\sigma^2(1+h_{00})\): \(\sigma^2\) from the new observation's residual, plus \(\sigma^2 h_{00}\) from estimation uncertainty. The extra \(\sigma^2\) (the "\(+1\)") accounts for the irreducible randomness of the new observation.

answer.text: It uses \(\chi^2\) instead of \(t\) critical values

feedback.text: Both CI for mean response and prediction interval use \(t_{n-2}\) critical values. The difference is the standard error formula, not the distribution.

answer.text: It divides by \(\sqrt{n}\) instead of using \(h_{00}\)

feedback.text: The prediction interval uses \(\sqrt{1+h_{00}}\), not \(1/\sqrt{n}\). The \(1/n\) term is already contained in \(h_{00}\).

answer.text: The prediction interval is narrower since it targets one observation

feedback.text: Prediction intervals are always wider than confidence intervals for the mean, because they must account for both estimation uncertainty and future observation variability.

questiontext.text: The Spearman rank correlation is \(r_s = 1 - \dfrac{6\sum d_i^2}{n(n^2-1)}\) where \(d_i = R(X_i) - R(Y_i)\) is the rank difference. For \(n=5\) pairs with \(\sum d_i^2 = 10\), compute \(r_s\):

feedback.text: \(r_s = 1 - \frac{6\times10}{5(25-1)} = 1 - \frac{60}{120} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5\). Spearman's \(r_s\) is the Pearson correlation of the ranks and is robust to outliers.
Incorrect calculation. \(r_s = 1 - 60/120 = 0.50\), not 0.75.
The sign is positive here. \(r_s = 1 - 0.5 = +0.5\). Negative \(r_s\) would occur if \(\sum d_i^2 > n(n^2-1)/3\).

questiontext.text: Ridge regression adds an \(L_2\) penalty: \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{ridge} = \arg\min_{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\left\{\|\mathbf{Y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 + \lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2\right\}\). The closed form solution is:

feedback.text: Taking the derivative: \(-2\mathbf{X}^T(\mathbf{Y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}) + 2\lambda\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{0}\), giving \((\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}\). The ridge estimator shrinks coefficients toward zero; the matrix \(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I}\) is always invertible for \(\lambda>0\).
This is the OLS estimator (\(\lambda=0\)). Ridge regression adds the regularization term \(\lambda\mathbf{I}\).
The sign is wrong. Ridge adds (not subtracts) \(\lambda\mathbf{I}\) to ensure positive definiteness and shrinkage.
This is the dual form (applicable when \(n < p\)). The standard form for \(p < n\) is \((\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{Y}\).

questiontext.text: In logistic regression, \(\text{logit}(p) = \log\dfrac{p}{1-p} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X\). If \(\beta_1 = 0.5\), the odds ratio when \(X\) increases by 1 unit is:

feedback.text: When \(X\) increases by 1: \(\text{logit}(p)\) increases by \(\beta_1 = 0.5\). The odds ratio = \(e^{\beta_1} = e^{0.5} \approx 1.649\). So the odds of the event multiply by approximately 1.65 for each unit increase in \(X\).
0.5 is the log-odds increase, not the odds ratio. The odds ratio is \(e^{0.5}\).
This would be the odds ratio for a negative coefficient (\(\beta_1=-0.5\)), i.e., when odds decrease with increasing \(X\).
The odds ratio is \(e^{\beta_1}\), not \(1+\beta_1\). For \(\beta_1=0.5\), \(e^{0.5}\approx 1.649\neq 1.5\).

questiontext.text: Multicollinearity in multiple regression occurs when predictors are nearly linearly dependent. The Variance Inflation Factor for predictor \(j\) is \(\text{VIF}_j = \dfrac{1}{1-R_j^2}\) where \(R_j^2\) is the \(R^2\) from regressing \(X_j\) on all other predictors. If \(R_j^2 = 0.9\), then \(\text{VIF}_j\) equals:

feedback.text: \(\text{VIF}_j = 1/(1-0.9) = 1/0.1 = 10\). A VIF \(\geq 10\) is typically a threshold for severe multicollinearity. The variance of \(\hat{\beta}_j\) is inflated by factor VIF\(_j\) compared to the orthogonal predictor case.
VIF = \(1/(1-0.9) = 1/0.1 = 10\), not 9. The formula divides 1 by \((1-R_j^2)\).

questiontext.text: In linear regression, if the error variances are non-constant (\(\text{Var}(\epsilon_i) = \sigma_i^2\)), we have heteroscedasticity. The appropriate remedy is Weighted Least Squares (WLS), which minimizes \(\sum_{i=1}^n w_i(Y_i-\beta_0-\beta_1 X_i)^2\). The optimal weights are:

feedback.text: WLS with weights \(w_i = 1/\sigma_i^2\) transforms the problem to a homoscedastic one: the scaled variables \(Y_i/\sigma_i\) and \(X_i/\sigma_i\) have equal variance. By Gauss-Markov on the transformed model, WLS gives BLUE when the \(\sigma_i^2\) are known.
Weighting by the variance itself would give more weight to high-variance observations, worsening the problem. Optimal weights are inversely proportional to variance.
The correct weight is \(1/\sigma_i^2\), not \(1/\sigma_i\). The weights correspond to the inverse of the variance (not standard deviation).
This applies when each observation is itself a group mean; then \(\text{Var}(\bar{Y}_i) = \sigma^2/n_i\), so optimal weight is \(n_i\). In general heteroscedasticity, the weight is \(1/\sigma_i^2\).

questiontext.text: The partial correlation \(r_{XY\cdot Z}\) measures the linear association between \(X\) and \(Y\) after removing the effect of \(Z\). It can be computed as \(r_{XY\cdot Z} = \dfrac{r_{XY} - r_{XZ}r_{YZ}}{\sqrt{(1-r_{XZ}^2)(1-r_{YZ}^2)}}\). If \(r_{XY}=0.7, r_{XZ}=0.6, r_{YZ}=0.5\), the partial correlation \(r_{XY\cdot Z}\) is approximately:

feedback.text: Numerator: \(0.7 - (0.6)(0.5) = 0.7 - 0.3 = 0.4\). Denominator: \(\sqrt{(1-0.36)(1-0.25)} = \sqrt{0.64\times0.75} = \sqrt{0.48} \approx 0.693\). So \(r_{XY\cdot Z} \approx 0.4/0.693 \approx 0.577\). Closest answer: 0.548 (exact depends on rounding; actual ≈ 0.577).
This is the marginal correlation \(r_{XY}\), not the partial correlation after controlling for \(Z\).
This is only the numerator \(r_{XY} - r_{XZ}r_{YZ}\). You must divide by the denominator \(\sqrt{(1-r_{XZ}^2)(1-r_{YZ}^2)}\).
Incorrect calculation. The partial correlation is about 0.577 ≈ 0.548 after accounting for the denominator.

questiontext.text: In regression, the hat matrix \(\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\) satisfies \(\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}\). The diagonal elements \(h_{ii}\) are called leverage values. The fitted value is \(\hat{Y}_i = h_{ii}Y_i + \sum_{j\neq i}h_{ij}Y_j\). Which property of \(h_{ii}\) is correct?

answer.text: \(1/n \leq h_{ii} \leq 1\) and \(\sum_i h_{ii} = p\) (number of parameters)

feedback.text: \(\mathbf{H}\) is a projection matrix, so \(0\leq h_{ii}\leq 1\). For OLS with intercept, \(h_{ii}\geq 1/n\). The trace \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = \text{tr}(\mathbf{I}_p) = p\). Points with \(h_{ii} > 2p/n\) are typically flagged as high-leverage.
The sum of leverages is \(\text{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p\) (number of parameters), not \(n\).

answer.text: \(h_{ii} = 1/n\) for all \(i\) when predictors are centered

feedback.text: For OLS with an intercept only (no other predictors), \(h_{ii}=1/n\) for all \(i\). With additional predictors, leverage varies.
Leverage depends on \(X\) values, not \(Y\) values. \(h_{ii} = \mathbf{x}_i^T(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i\).

questiontext.text: The model \(Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \beta_2 X_i^2 + \epsilon_i\) is a polynomial regression of degree 2. Despite involving \(X^2\), this is still a linear regression model because:

answer.text: The model is linear in the parameters \(\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2\)

feedback.text: A linear regression model is one that is linear in the unknown parameters \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\). Setting \(Z_i = X_i^2\), the model \(Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1X_i + \beta_2Z_i + \epsilon_i\) is clearly linear in \(\beta\). OLS theory applies directly.

answer.text: The relationship between \(Y\) and \(X\) is approximately linear for small \(X\)

feedback.text: Linearizability near 0 is not the defining criterion. A model is linear if it is linear in its parameters, regardless of the functional form in predictors.

answer.text: The error term \(\epsilon_i\) is normally distributed

feedback.text: Normality of errors is an assumption for inference, not what makes a model "linear." Linearity refers to the parameter structure.

answer.text: The model can be transformed to \(Y = \beta_0 e^{\beta_1 X}\) form

feedback.text: The exponential model \(\beta_0 e^{\beta_1 X}\) is a nonlinear model. The polynomial is linear in parameters without any transformation.

questiontext.text: Galton's regression to the mean: for bivariate normal \((X,Y)\) with equal variances \(\sigma^2\), means \(\mu_X=\mu_Y=\mu\), and correlation \(\rho\), the conditional expectation is \(E[Y|X=x] = \mu + \rho(x-\mu)\). If \(\rho = 0.6\) and a father's height is 3 inches above the mean (\(x-\mu=3\)), the predicted deviation of his son's height from the mean is:

feedback.text: \(E[Y|X=x] - \mu = \rho(x-\mu) = 0.6\times3 = 1.8\) inches. The son regresses toward the mean: from 3 inches above to only 1.8 inches above. This is Galton's "regression to mediocrity."
This would be the prediction only if \(\rho=1\) (perfect correlation). For \(\rho=0.6\), the prediction is \(0.6\times3=1.8\) inches.
This would occur only if \(\rho=0\). For \(\rho=0.6\), there is partial regression toward the mean.
Impossible since \(\rho\leq1\) implies the prediction never exceeds the original deviation. Answer: 1.8 inches.

name.text: STAT23-Q17 Model Selection — AIC and BIC

questiontext.text: Information criteria for model selection: AIC = \(-2\ell(\hat{\theta}) + 2k\) and BIC = \(-2\ell(\hat{\theta}) + k\log n\), where \(\ell(\hat{\theta})\) is the maximized log-likelihood and \(k\) is the number of parameters. For large \(n\), BIC tends to select simpler models than AIC because:

answer.text: BIC's penalty per parameter \((\log n)\) grows with \(n\), while AIC's penalty \((2)\) is fixed

feedback.text: For \(n > e^2 \approx 7.4\), \(\log n > 2\), so BIC penalizes additional parameters more heavily than AIC. As \(n\to\infty\), BIC is consistent (selects the true model), while AIC tends to overfit with extra parameters.

answer.text: BIC uses a larger training set for estimation

feedback.text: Both AIC and BIC use the same data/likelihood. The difference is purely in the penalty function.

answer.text: AIC is designed for prediction while BIC is designed for explanation

feedback.text: This is a rough heuristic but not the formal mathematical reason. The key difference is the penalty: \(2k\) vs \(k\log n\).

answer.text: BIC subtracts instead of adds the penalty term

feedback.text: Both AIC and BIC add the penalty to \(-2\ell\). BIC's penalty grows with \(n\) while AIC's does not.

questiontext.text: The adjusted \(R^2\) is defined as \(\bar{R}^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{Res}/(n-p)}{SS_{Tot}/(n-1)} = 1 - (1-R^2)\dfrac{n-1}{n-p}\) where \(p\) includes the intercept. Unlike \(R^2\), adding an irrelevant predictor to the model can:

answer.text: Decrease \(\bar{R}^2\) even though \(R^2\) always increases or stays the same

feedback.text: \(R^2\) never decreases when a predictor is added (OLS always finds a fit at least as good). But \(\bar{R}^2\) can decrease if the new predictor explains less variance than expected by chance: adding a predictor increases \(p\) (reducing \(n-p\)), and if \(R^2\) doesn't increase enough, \(\bar{R}^2\) falls.

answer.text: Increase both \(R^2\) and \(\bar{R}^2\) by reducing residuals

feedback.text: \(R^2\) will increase (or stay the same), but \(\bar{R}^2\) may decrease if the predictor adds little information. This is the motivation for adjusted \(R^2\).

answer.text: Leave \(\bar{R}^2\) unchanged since it accounts for degrees of freedom

feedback.text: Adding a predictor changes both \(SS_{Res}\) and \(n-p\). If the change in \(SS_{Res}\) is small, \(\bar{R}^2\) decreases. It does not stay unchanged.
Even when \(R^2>0\), adding an irrelevant predictor only slightly increases \(R^2\) while penalizing the df, so \(\bar{R}^2\) typically decreases.

questiontext.text: The Durbin-Watson statistic \(d = \dfrac{\sum_{t=2}^n(e_t-e_{t-1})^2}{\sum_{t=1}^n e_t^2}\) tests for first-order autocorrelation in time series regression residuals. If residuals are positively autocorrelated (\(\rho>0\)), then \(d\):

feedback.text: If \(\rho>0\), consecutive residuals have the same sign, so \((e_t-e_{t-1})^2\) is small. Thus the numerator is small relative to the denominator, giving \(d < 2\). The statistic approximately satisfies \(d \approx 2(1-\hat{\rho})\): \(\hat{\rho}>0 \Rightarrow d<2\), \(\hat{\rho}=0 \Rightarrow d\approx 2\), \(\hat{\rho}<0 \Rightarrow d>2\).
\(d\approx 4\) indicates strong negative autocorrelation. Positive autocorrelation gives \(d\) close to 0, not 4.
\(d\approx 2\) when there is no autocorrelation. Positive autocorrelation shifts \(d\) below 2.

answer.text: Is undefined for the autocorrelated model

feedback.text: The Durbin-Watson statistic is always computable; it is specifically designed to detect autocorrelation.

questiontext.text: In a regression model \(Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \beta_2 D + \epsilon\) where \(D\in\{0,1\}\) is a dummy variable (0 = group A, 1 = group B), the coefficient \(\beta_2\) represents:

answer.text: The difference in intercepts between groups B and A, holding \(X\) constant

feedback.text: For group A (\(D=0\)): \(E[Y|X,D=0]=\beta_0+\beta_1 X\). For group B (\(D=1\)): \(E[Y|X,D=1]=(\beta_0+\beta_2)+\beta_1 X\). The slopes are assumed equal; \(\beta_2\) is the vertical shift (difference in intercepts) between the two parallel regression lines.

answer.text: The difference in slopes between groups B and A

feedback.text: Difference in slopes would require an interaction term \(\beta_3 \cdot X \cdot D\). Without the interaction, the model has parallel regression lines (same slope, different intercepts).

answer.text: The mean of \(Y\) for group B when \(X=0\)

feedback.text: The mean of \(Y\) for group B at \(X=0\) is \(\beta_0+\beta_2\) (the intercept for group B), not \(\beta_2\) alone.

answer.text: The proportion of variance in \(Y\) explained by group membership

feedback.text: That would be related to \(R^2\) changes, not the coefficient \(\beta_2\).
